notes.note: Epicurus (341-270 BC), the Greek philosopher whose school Fadius Gallus followed; Cicero teases his Epicurean friend by recalling that the Stoics mocked Epicurus for admitting that bladder and bowel complaints (the price of the high life) distressed him.

Caesar's sumptuary law of 46 BC capped spending on luxury foods (oysters, lampreys, imported delicacies) but exempted produce of the soil. Cicero's joke is that the law backfired: cooks lavished their art on the permitted vegetables, and the rich greens at the dinner gave him worse trouble than any banned dish.

A cena auguralis, the lavish banquet a newly co-opted member of the college of augurs gave to mark his inauguration. The host is conventionally identified as P. Cornelius Lentulus Spinther the younger.
C. Anicius, a senatorial friend of Cicero, who witnessed Cicero's bout of sickness and reported it to Gallus.
